## Memo: Rewrite vs. Incremental Refactor of the Legacy PHP Monolith

**Recommendation: Refactor incrementally using the Strangler Fig pattern. Do not do a big-bang rewrite** — unless the monolith meets one of the three disqualifying conditions below, in which case rewrite a *bounded module*, never the whole system at once.

### Why incremental wins by default

A full rewrite restarts the clock on years of accumulated bug fixes and edge-case handling that live in the code but in nobody's head. During the rewrite you either freeze features (the business stalls and competitors don't) or maintain two systems in parallel (double the work, guaranteed drift). Rewrites also routinely overrun: the "we'll be done in six months" estimate is made against an idealized spec, not against the real, undocumented behavior the old system actually delivers. Incremental refactoring keeps the system shippable the entire time and lets you stop, reprioritize, or bank partial value at any point — a rewrite is all-or-nothing until the switchover.

### When a rewrite is actually the right call

Rewrite only if one or more is clearly true:
1. **The runtime/platform is a dead end** — e.g. stuck on an unsupported PHP version with no upgrade path, or a framework that's abandoned and blocks security patching.
2. **The architecture physically blocks the business** — you cannot scale, cannot meet a hard compliance requirement, or every new feature takes 5x longer than it should because of a structural flaw that refactoring can't reach.
3. **The domain itself has fundamentally changed** — what the system needs to do now is so different from what it was built for that you're not improving it, you're replacing the problem.

Cost, "it's ugly," and developer dislike are *not* on this list. Those are refactor problems.

### Recommended path (Strangler Fig)

1. **Stop the bleeding first:** add a test harness / characterization tests around current behavior, get it into CI, and put a reverse proxy or routing layer in front of the monolith so traffic can be redirected per-route.
2. **Carve at the seams:** pick the highest-pain, lowest-coupling module (often auth, billing, or a reporting subsystem). Extract it behind a clean interface — new code can be a service, a separated module, or modern PHP, whatever fits.
3. **Route and verify:** send that slice's traffic to the new implementation, keep the old path as a fallback, compare outputs in production before cutting over.
4. **Repeat, prioritizing by pain × risk.** The monolith shrinks; you ship continuously; you can stop anytime with value already realized.

### What would change this recommendation

Tell me which of these is true and I'll sharpen the call:
- **PHP version & framework** (in support, or end-of-life?)
- **Test coverage today** (near zero changes the sequencing — characterization tests come first)
- **Team size and whether you can run two tracks** (a small team makes big-bang rewrite even more dangerous)
- **Is there a hard external deadline or compliance driver** forcing a platform change?

The default holds across almost all of these: **incremental, seam-by-seam, with the monolith live the whole way.** The honest one-line version is that "rewrite from scratch" feels faster and is almost always slower — the risk is the cost.

If you want, I can turn this into a saved doc, or rework it against your actual stack once you give me the four facts above.

## Memo: Rewrite vs. Incremental Refactor — Legacy PHP Monolith

**Decision requested:** Approval to adopt an incremental refactor (Strangler Fig) as the default modernization path, with full rewrites scoped to individual modules only when they clear the bar defined below.

**Recommendation:** Refactor incrementally. Do not attempt a big-bang rewrite of the whole system. Where a rewrite is justified, rewrite one bounded module at a time — never the entire monolith at once.

**Bottom line:** A from-scratch rewrite feels faster and is almost always slower. The risk *is* the cost.

### Why incremental wins by default

A full rewrite restarts the clock on years of accumulated bug fixes and edge-case handling that live in the code but in no one's head. During the rewrite you either freeze features — the business stalls while competitors don't — or you maintain two systems in parallel, which doubles the work and guarantees drift. Rewrites also routinely overrun, because the "done in six months" estimate is made against an idealized spec rather than the real, undocumented behavior the old system actually delivers. Incremental refactoring keeps the system shippable the entire time and lets you stop, reprioritize, or bank partial value at any point. A rewrite is all-or-nothing until the switchover.

### When a rewrite is the right call

Rewrite a module only if at least one of these is clearly true:

1. **The runtime or platform is a dead end** — e.g. an unsupported PHP version with no upgrade path, or an abandoned framework that blocks security patching.
2. **The architecture physically blocks the business** — you cannot scale, cannot meet a hard compliance requirement, or every new feature costs several times what it should because of a structural flaw refactoring cannot reach.
3. **The domain has fundamentally changed** — what the system must do now is so different from what it was built for that you're not improving it, you're replacing the problem.

Cost, "it's ugly," and developer dislike are not on this list. Those are refactor problems.

### Recommended path (Strangler Fig)

1. **Stop the bleeding.** Add characterization tests around current behavior, get them into CI, and put a routing layer (reverse proxy) in front of the monolith so traffic can be redirected per route.
2. **Carve at the seams.** Pick the highest-pain, lowest-coupling module — often auth, billing, or reporting — and extract it behind a clean interface. The new implementation can be a service, a separated module, or modern PHP, whatever fits.
3. **Route and verify.** Send that slice's traffic to the new implementation, keep the old path as a fallback, and compare outputs against production before cutting over.
4. **Repeat, prioritizing by pain × risk.** The monolith shrinks, you ship continuously, and you can stop at any point with value already realized.

### The main risk of this path — and how we contain it

Strangler projects can stall: you carve out two or three modules, urgent work returns, and you're left maintaining a hybrid indefinitely. We contain this by (a) starting only modules we're committed to finishing, (b) tracking "share of endpoints/traffic off the monolith" as a standing metric reviewed each cycle, and (c) deleting old code paths once a slice is cut over, so the migration cannot quietly reverse.

### Inputs that refine sequencing (not the recommendation)

The recommendation holds regardless of the answers below — these shape ordering and staffing, not the decision:

- **PHP version and framework** — in support, or end-of-life? (An EOL runtime promotes specific modules to rewrite candidates under condition 1.)
- **Current test coverage** — near-zero means characterization tests come first and dominate the early schedule.
- **Team size and capacity for two tracks** — a small team makes a big-bang rewrite even more dangerous and argues for fewer modules in flight at once.
- **Any hard external deadline or compliance driver** forcing a platform change — this sets the order of carving, not the choice between approaches.
